metadata.title: Guideline on Clinical Evaluation of Medicinal Products Used in Weight Control — Addendum on Weight Control in Children
metadata.document_type: Finalized Guideline (Addendum to CPMP/EWP/281/96 Rev.1; in effect 1 February 2009)

rules.rule_text: Age- and sex-adjusted BMI cut-offs based on pooled international data (linking adult cut-offs of 25 kg/m² for overweight and 30 kg/m² for obesity to age- and sex-specific paediatric cut-offs) must be used to define overweight and obesity in paediatric clinical trials; the adult flat cut-off of 30 kg/m² is not acceptable for children.
rules.source_section: Section 1. Definition of Obesity in Children
rules.verbatim_quote: BMI cut-offs based on pooled international data that link the accepted adult cut-off points (a BMI of 25 Kg/m2 for overweight and 30 Kg/m2 for obesity) to cut-off points related to age and sex for children should be used to define overweight and obesity in the paediatric population.

rules.rule_text: For children aged 2 to 6 years, weight loss must be achieved through lifestyle modification only; pharmacological interventions are not appropriate in this age group.
rules.verbatim_quote: For children aged 2 years to 6 years it is recommended that weight loss be attained through lifestyle modification only.

rules.rule_text: Studies in children are required for paediatric indications; in general, adult study results cannot be extrapolated to adolescents, nor adolescent results to younger children. Extra-European data may only be extrapolated when it is established that response is not dependent on ethnic background, with justification provided.
rules.verbatim_quote: In general the results of studies in adults cannot be extrapolated to adolescents or from adolescents to younger children. Study results from weight loss trials outside Europe could, however, be extrapolated to the European population when it is established that the response to the therapy is not dependent on any ethnic background. Justification should be provided for using extrapolation data.
rules.exceptions: Non-European data may be used with justified evidence of ethnic independence of response.

rules.rule_text: Clinical trials in paediatric obesity must include and precisely define all types of non-pharmacological intervention (lifestyle changes, diet, physical activity, parental involvement); these interventions must begin in the run-in phase and continue unchanged through the treatment and follow-up phases.
rules.verbatim_quote: Design of clinical trials on obesity in children should include, and precisely define, all types of intervention (lifestyle changes, diet, physical activity, parental involvement). All non-pharmacological interventions should begin in the run-in phase and continue during the blind treatment phase and during the follow-up phase.

rules.rule_text: Separate pivotal trials must be conducted for pre-pubertal children (age 6 years to onset of puberty, defined by Tanner score 2) and post-pubertal children (post-puberty to age 18 years), due to significant physiological differences in body composition, metabolic responses, and behaviour occurring during puberty.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is recommended that separate trials for pre-pubertal (6 years to puberty) versus post-pubertal children (post-puberty to 18 years) are performed. This is because of the considerable physiological changes in body composition, metabolic responses and behaviour occurring during puberty.

rules.rule_text: Patients enrolled in paediatric obesity trials must be obese and must have a documented history of failing to lose weight through lifestyle modification before entering the pharmacological phase.
rules.source_section: Section 3.4. Design of clinical studies — Inclusion criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients should be obese and have a documented history of failing to lose weight by means of lifestyle modification, before enrolment into the pharmacological phase of a study.
rules.exceptions: Children or adolescents with severe obesity and at least one co-morbidity should enter the active phase irrespective of run-in weight changes.

rules.rule_text: Patients with secondary causes of childhood obesity (mental retardation, chromosomal problems, syndromic obesity such as Prader-Willi syndrome) must be excluded from pivotal trials; separate trials are required for this population. Patients who have undergone bariatric surgery and those with confirmed bulimia nervosa must also be excluded.
rules.source_section: Section 3.4. Design of clinical studies — Exclusion criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients with secondary causes of childhood obesity such as mental retardation, chromosomal problems or syndromic obesity (e.g. Prader-Willi syndrome) should be excluded from the pivotal trials. Separate trials are needed for children with secondary causes of obesity. Patients who have undergone any surgical intervention for the management of obesity e.g. bariatric surgery should also be excluded from a trial. Patients with confirmed bulimia nervosa disorder should be excluded from trials.

rules.rule_text: Paediatric obesity trials must be randomised, double-blind, and placebo-controlled, comprising three sequential phases: a run-in phase (3 to 6 months without medication), a blinded active treatment phase (minimum 1 year), and a follow-up phase (minimum 6 months after discontinuation).
rules.verbatim_quote: Trials should be randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials. There should be a run-in phase, followed after randomisation by a blinded treatment phase and follow-up. The run-in period (without any medication) should last 3 to 6 months. The treatment phase should last for at least 1 year. It is recommended that the observation phase after stopping drug therapy should last 6 months at least.
rules.exceptions: Studies of shortened duration require scientific advice.

rules.rule_text: All paediatric obesity trial participants, including dropouts, must be included in the intention-to-treat analysis; every effort must be made to follow up patients who withdraw, including obtaining body weight measurements at the time they would have completed the 1-year study.
rules.verbatim_quote: Every effort should be made to follow-up these patients fully and include them in the intention to treat analysis. If possible, patients who drop out from a 1 year study should have a body weight measurement at the time he or she would have completed the study after 1 year of taking part.

rules.rule_text: Body composition analysis using a validated methodology must be performed in a representative sample of trial patients to confirm that any weight reduction is caused primarily by a reduction in fat content rather than lean-body mass.
rules.verbatim_quote: Body composition analysis using a validated methodology is necessary in a representative sample of trial patients to ensure that any weight reduction is caused primarily by a reduction in fat content and not lean-body mass.

rules.rule_text: The primary efficacy endpoint in paediatric obesity trials must be change in BMI Standard Deviation Score (BMI-SDS); change in adiposity expressed in BMI (%) units should also be measured. The degree of clinically meaningful change must be justified by the applicant, as the adult threshold of 10% weight reduction is not automatically applicable.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is recommended that the primary endpoint is a change in BMI Standard Deviation Score; however, change in adiposity should also be measured in BMI (%) units. In adults a 10% weight reduction is accepted as a clinically meaningful effect. However, in the paediatric population the degree of change should be justified by the applicant.
rules.exceptions: Halting abnormal/excess weight gain or decreasing the rate of weight gain are also acceptable primary endpoints where weight loss is not the primary goal.
cross_references: Cole et al. Eur J Clin Nut 2005

rules.rule_text: Behavioural changes must be assessed as secondary endpoints using validated psychological tools; justification for the choice of tools must be provided.
rules.verbatim_quote: Behavioural changes are considered as secondary endpoints. Validated psychological tools must be used and justification for their use should be provided.

rules.rule_text: Safety data must be collected throughout the entire drug treatment period (minimum 1 year) and the follow-up period, encompassing adverse events related to lipid profile, liver function, cardiovascular system function, and rebound phenomenon.
rules.verbatim_quote: Appropriate safety data should be collected during the entire drug treatment period, which will usually be at least one year, and for the duration of the follow-up period. This data should notably encompass the adverse events related to lipid profile, liver function, cardiovascular system function and rebound phenomenon.

rules.rule_text: For centrally-acting anorectic agents, special monitoring of neuropsychiatric events must be incorporated into the trial design, including depression, sleep patterns, nightmares, self-esteem, aggression, and suicidality.
rules.verbatim_quote: For centrally-acting anorectic agents, in particular, it is recommended that special attention and monitoring is afforded to neuropsychiatric events such as depression, sleep pattern and nightmares, assessment of self-esteem, aggression or suicidality.
rules.exceptions: Applies specifically to centrally-acting anorectic agents.

rules.rule_text: In growing children, height velocity must be monitored throughout the trial in addition to standard safety evaluations; pubertal development must be assessed by Tanner stage at baseline and endpoint.
rules.verbatim_quote: In growing children height velocity should also be monitored in addition to standard safety evaluations and pubertal development should be assessed by determining Tanner stage at baseline and endpoint.

rules.rule_text: For centrally-acting anorectic agents, the abuse potential must be evaluated and factored into trial design, with particular attention to the adolescent age group.
rules.verbatim_quote: For centrally-acting anorectic agents the abuse potential should be considered and factored into trial design in an appropriate manner. This is especially important in the adolescent age group.
rules.exceptions: Applies specifically to centrally-acting anorectic agents.
